segments.title: Japan's first destroyers as force-structure tools
segments.summary: The lecture frames Japan's first destroyers as part of a balanced fleet plan that built domestic naval industry while phasing beyond torpedo boats.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke links the 6-6 fleet plan to Japan's need for sea power, then explains how early destroyer and torpedo-boat procurement spread construction skills, demanded high quality, and gave Japan a more flexible light-force tool than torpedo boats alone.

evidence.note: The transcript says destroyers and torpedo boats were used to build Japanese infrastructure and industry while spreading construction and keeping quality standards.
The surrounding passage connects Japan's coastline, survival concerns, balanced fleet ambitions, and first destroyer orders with a 12-destroyer force-structure start.
The transcript explains that destroyers fit Japanese doctrine better than torpedo boats because they carried both guns and torpedoes and could fight multiple light-force threats.
